title: Folklore the Affliction: Fall of the Spire - Part 3: Adventure Recap and Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4zfnW56TcvY

First recap of Story One, Chapter One — a Jack-the-Ripper-style mystery in Eürotrusk's back alleys. Daniel (@dungeondive) and his femme-fatale party (Druidus + Huntress) hunt five lettered compass tokens across the city, fight cutthroats and a gargoyle, and close on the *Anatomist* — a weakened foreshadowing of the full-game affliction boss. The Druidus, hilariously, searches up a hand cannon: a robed earth-binder priest now armed with a sawed-off shotgun.

Most of the video is reserved for the expansion's headline new mechanic — the **initiative track**. Each hero rolls a d10 at the start of every adventure to place on a 1–10 ladder. Two initiative-bonus cards then bind to numbered slots (in 2-player mode they upgrade in value to compensate for the smaller party). Foes get their own bonus cards. Spending power points lets you slide up or down the track to claim a slot. Daniel loves it — wants more dungeon crawls to do this — but quietly wishes the slot-shift cost was an action rather than power.

Two other expansion additions also surface: the **town events** deck (a thoroughfare/back-alley push-your-luck draw when you stay in town) and the **rumors** deck — a stack of side quests Daniel calls one of his favourite things in any dungeon crawl. He pulls *Death and Decay* on his inn-stay draw.

----

The Folklore initiative track is random per-encounter — you can spend power to slide up or down, but the *positions* themselves are fixed at draw. Random-then-finesseable: does that hit a better spot than fully-deterministic or fully-random initiative?
